refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate streaming with forward error correction (FEC) and automatic codec switching based on network quality metrics.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (primary + backup) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-C: Use application-level acknowledgment and selective retransmission of critical telemetry and command packets.
DP-D: Implement network quality monitoring with dynamic traffic prioritization (QoS) to protect safety-critical packets.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-D (Integrated approach): Implement adaptive bitrate streaming with forward error correction and automatic codec switching (DP-A) combined with continuous network quality monitoring and dynamic QoS-based traffic prioritization (DP-D). This dual strategy addresses both the root cause (network variability) and the symptom (packet loss) while maintaining operator responsiveness and safety criticality.

potential_sources: DP-A couples to network bandwidth availability; DP-D couples to network infrastructure QoS capabilities.
Combined approach may introduce complexity in codec negotiation and QoS policy management.
Decoupling achieved by separating concerns: DP-A handles encoding/transmission resilience; DP-D handles network-layer prioritization.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP combination is loosely coupled. DP-A operates at the application/codec layer; DP-D operates at the network/QoS layer. Both are necessary and complementary: DP-A ensures data integrity under loss; DP-D reduces loss occurrence. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer network infrastructure supports QoS tagging and prioritization (DP-D requirement).
Assumption: Operator hardware (console) supports adaptive codec switching without perceptible latency increase.
Risk: FEC overhead may increase bandwidth consumption, potentially worsening packet loss on bandwidth-constrained links.
Risk: Codec switching latency could introduce command delays during network transitions.
Risk: QoS policies may conflict with other site network traffic; coordination with IT/network team required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to specify maximum acceptable latency overhead from FEC and codec switching (e.g., ≤100ms added latency). Refine DP-D to include fallback behavior if QoS is unavailable on customer network. Add explicit assumption validation gate: confirm customer network QoS capability before detailed design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication pipeline, including codec selection, adaptive bitrate management, FEC implementation, network quality monitoring, QoS policy enforcement, and real-time packet loss reporting. Interfaces with Vehicle for command transmission and feedback reception; interfaces with Safety Services for packet loss alerts and operational status.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from remote operator console; must be resilient to packet loss via FEC and retransmission logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and video feed to operator console; subject to adaptive bitrate and codec switching based on network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports real-time packet loss metrics and network quality degradation to Safety Services; triggers alerts if packet loss exceeds threshold.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (WiFi, cellular, hybrid); Teleoperation module monitors link quality and adapts transmission strategy accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own packet loss management. Packet loss is a teleoperation-specific concern; assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple subsystems.

Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and local control but does not own communication pipeline. Assigning packet loss management to Vehicle would couple communication concerns with vehicle mechanics, violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. All DP elements (FEC, codec switching, QoS, monitoring) are teleoperation-specific. High cohesion; minimal diffusion. This is the correct assignment.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting but does not own the communication mechanism itself. Packet loss management is a communication concern, not a safety policy concern. Safety Services should consume packet loss alerts from Teleoperation, not own the mechanism.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of packet loss management because: (1) all technical mechanisms (FEC, codec switching, QoS) are teleoperation-specific; (2) the module has direct control over the communication pipeline; (3) assignment minimizes diffusion and maximizes cohesion; (4) interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) are already owned by or flow through Teleoperation.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become overloaded if it also owns operator console UI and command parsing. Mitigation: ensure clear separation between communication layer (packet loss management) and application layer (UI/command parsing).

Risk: Network Link interface may be shared with other NGHE subsystems (e.g., diagnostics, firmware updates). Mitigation: establish QoS policies that protect teleoperation traffic without starving other critical functions.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time packet loss data for safety decisions. Mitigation: ensure SafetyAlert interface provides low-latency, high-reliability packet loss reporting (possibly via separate safety channel).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation module: separate the communication transport layer (FEC, codec, QoS) from the command/feedback application layer. Define explicit sub-module boundaries (e.g., 'Teleoperation.Transport', 'Teleoperation.CommandHandler') to prevent diffusion. Establish SLA for SafetyAlert interface: packet loss alerts must be delivered within 500ms of detection.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (adaptive bitrate + FEC + QoS monitoring) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. DP-A (FEC and codec switching) reduces packet loss occurrence; DP-D (QoS monitoring) prevents loss by prioritizing teleoperation traffic. The combination is necessary and sufficient to meet the acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions. No gaps or misalignments detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: If Teleoperation module also owns operator console UI, codec negotiation logic may diffuse across UI and transport layers. Mitigation: enforce strict layering within Teleoperation module.
Low risk: QoS policy enforcement may require coordination with network infrastructure outside NGHE Solution. Mitigation: document network configuration assumptions and establish handoff points.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the correct owner with high containment score (0.95). All DP mechanisms are teleoperation-specific and can be cohesively implemented within this module. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and do not require additional module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via network simulation and live testing. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end using standard network tools (packet capture, loss injection). Acceptance criterion (≤1% under agreed conditions) is objective and measurable. Test plan should include: (1) baseline network conditions, (2) degraded conditions (WiFi interference, cellular congestion), (3) failover scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss management; Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement (e.g., operator alert if packet loss exceeds threshold for >5 seconds); Vehicle owns command execution. Responsibilities do not overlap.

recommended_revisions: Add explicit verification gate: confirm customer network QoS capability before proceeding to detailed design. If QoS unavailable, revise DP to rely solely on DP-A (FEC + codec switching) without DP-D.
Define latency budget: specify maximum acceptable latency overhead from FEC and codec switching (recommend ≤100ms). Add latency verification to test plan.
Establish interface SLA for SafetyAlert: packet loss alerts must be delivered within 500ms of detection to enable timely operator intervention.
Document fallback behavior: if packet loss exceeds threshold despite DP implementation, define operator notification and system behavior (e.g., reduce vehicle speed, trigger safety shutdown).

decision.justification: The FR is well-refined and the DP is sound, but the requirement is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, protocol/codec selection, monitoring/reporting) are distinct concerns that should be decomposed into separate design branches. Additionally, the high safety criticality of teleoperation demands explicit allocation of safety-related sub-requirements (e.g., operator alerting, failover behavior) to Safety Services. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership boundaries and reduce integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms (FEC, selective retransmission) to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety services.

FR-NGHE-009.4 (Safety-critical): The NGHE solution shall alert the operator and safety manager if teleoperation packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold for more than 5 consecutive seconds, and shall initiate safe shutdown if packet loss persists for >10 seconds.

assumptions: Customer network infrastructure supports QoS tagging and prioritization (or DP-D can be deferred to Phase 2).
Operator hardware (console) supports adaptive codec switching without perceptible latency increase (≤100ms).
Agreed network conditions are defined and documented by customer before detailed design (e.g., WiFi 5GHz, cellular LTE, hybrid failover).
Packet loss is measured end-to-end (operator console → vehicle → operator console), not just one direction.
Safety Services module is available to consume SafetyAlert interface and enforce operator alerting policies.
open_risks: FEC overhead may increase bandwidth consumption, potentially worsening packet loss on bandwidth-constrained links. Mitigation: conduct bandwidth analysis during detailed design; establish FEC overhead budget (recommend ≤20% of baseline bandwidth).
Codec switching latency could introduce command delays during network transitions. Mitigation: specify maximum latency overhead (≤100ms); test codec switching under realistic network conditions.
QoS policies may conflict with other site network traffic. Mitigation: coordinate with customer IT/network team; establish QoS priority levels for NGHE vs. other site systems.

Operator may not perceive packet loss if it is masked by FEC/retransmission, leading to false confidence in network quality. Mitigation: ensure real-time packet loss metrics are visible to operator; establish alert thresholds.
evidence_needed: Customer network topology and QoS capability assessment (required before detailed design).
Baseline packet loss measurements under agreed network conditions (required for acceptance testing).
Codec switching latency measurements on target operator hardware (required for latency budget validation).
FEC overhead analysis and bandwidth impact assessment (required for network design).
Safety policy documentation: operator alerting thresholds, failover behavior, shutdown criteria (required for FR-NGHE-009.4 decomposition).
